The way out is not to stop being kind. It is to stop being nice—to reclaim the courage to tell the truth, the love that risks conflict, the care that names harm. It is to build a world where feedback flows, where the vulnerable can speak, where power cannot insulate itself from reality.

This is not a comfortable path. It will require confrontation. It will require conflict. It will require being willing to displease, to disrupt, to name what is hidden.

But it is the only path that leads to love.

The other path—the path of niceness—leads to a world of pleasant surfaces and rotting interiors, of smiles that hide suffering, of harmony that masks injustice. It leads to the peace of the graveyard, where everyone is polite because everyone is dead.

We can choose otherwise. We can choose to be real.

For those who have told me the truth when it would have been easier to be nice: I am alive because of you. For those who have risked my displeasure to serve my good: I am grateful beyond words. For those who have loved me enough to confront me: you have given me the only gift that matters.

May we learn to do the same for others.

Historical Patterns

A Gentle Inquiry into the Fusion of Power and Apocalyptic Purpose in Western Expansion

A Crossroads of Disciplines: Mapping the Fields This Paper Engages

Before presenting the revised framework, we must situate this investigation within the academic landscape it traverses. Like the previous papers, this one does not belong to a single discipline but stands at the intersection of multiple fields. Its central contribution is not the discovery of new historical facts but the articulation of a pattern-based lens for re-examining familiar history—a lens that reveals repeating structures across scales and centuries.

1. The Macro-Historical Framework: Jiang Xueqin and Structural Pattern Analysis

The paper’s methodological orientation finds a significant parallel in the work of Sino-Canadian historian and geopolitical theorist Jiang Xueqin. Jiang, a Yale-educated scholar based in Beijing, has developed a framework he calls “Predictive History” through his popular YouTube channel of the same name. His approach explicitly applies “structural historical analysis, game theory, and concepts inspired by Isaac Asimov’s fictional psychohistory” to interpret and predict major geopolitical developments.

Jiang’s method rests on a fundamental premise that aligns with this paper’s orientation: recurring historical patterns, when properly understood, reveal the underlying structures that drive human events across different eras and contexts. He argues that “both historical patterns and game theory can be used to accurately predict future events” because the same structural dynamics tend to produce similar outcomes under similar conditions.

What makes Jiang’s framework particularly relevant to this inquiry is his attention to the deep, macro-historical forces that shape civilizations. His lecture series “Civilization” traces “the narrative and ideological evolution of human societies from prehistory to the contemporary period, with particular emphasis on the unifying and mobilizing power of transcendent religious and civilizational belief systems”. This focus on how belief systems mobilize populations aligns directly with this paper’s examination of apocalyptic and quasi-religious narratives as drivers of expansion.

Jiang’s analysis of contemporary geopolitics draws heavily on historical parallels. In his widely discussed 2024 lecture, he compared a potential US-Iran conflict to the Athenian Sicilian Expedition during the Peloponnesian War—“a massive, ambitious military campaign launched by Athens that ultimately ended in total catastrophe”. This methodological move—using deep historical patterns to illuminate present dynamics—exemplifies the pattern-based approach this paper advocates.

This paper draws on Jiang’s framework to situate its own pattern-based analysis within a broader scholarly conversation about macro-historical structures. Jiang’s work demonstrates that pattern recognition, when done rigorously, can generate testable hypotheses about geopolitical outcomes—two of his three major 2024 predictions (Trump’s return to power and a US-Iran conflict) have already been borne out.

2. The Steppe Hypothesis: Geographical Migration as Pattern Generator

Your extension of the framework—that “the European pattern was shaped by a continuous stream of steppes people invading Europe, based on geographical migration”—engages a substantial body of scholarship on the role of Eurasian steppe nomads in shaping European civilization. This is a macro-historical pattern operating at an even deeper temporal scale than the Crusades-to-present arc this paper primarily examines.

The geographical determinism implicit in this hypothesis finds theoretical grounding in Jiang’s work, which acknowledges “geographical determinism” as one of his analytical frameworks. The argument would be that Europe’s historical trajectory cannot be understood without accounting for the repeated influx of steppe peoples—Scythians, Huns, Avars, Magyars, Mongols, and others—whose migrations shaped European political geography, military technology, and cultural development over millennia.

This pattern operates at what might be called the tectonic scale of history. Just as geological forces shape the terrain on which human events unfold, the centuries-long pressure of steppe migrations created conditions that predisposed European societies toward certain responses—fortification, militarization, and eventually, outward expansion as a release valve for internal pressures. The crusading impulse that this paper identifies as a template for later expansion may itself be understood as a response, in part, to the deep historical memory of invasion from the East.

This paper acknowledges this deeper pattern while focusing on the more recent fusion of power and meaning that characterized Western expansion. The steppe hypothesis reminds us that the patterns we observe at one scale are often themselves expressions of patterns operating at larger scales—a recursive insight that Jiang’s framework, with its emphasis on structural analysis across time, is well-equipped to accommodate.

3. Historiography and the Revisionist Tradition

The paper engages most directly with the revisionist tradition in American historiography. Scholars have long moved beyond the celebratory narratives of nineteenth-century historians who “glorified expansionism as a heroic struggle, a romantic adventure, a benevolent mission, and a Darwinian right or inevitability”. Postcolonial and revisionist historians have instead “asserted a more critical lens,” revealing that “viewed from the perspective of the conquered and colonized, the United States has been in a continual imperial mode since its colonial status, pushing ever westward.”

Robert J. Miller’s scholarship on the Doctrine of Discovery provides crucial legal-historical grounding for this revisionist perspective. Miller demonstrates that Manifest Destiny arose directly from the legal principles and elements of the Doctrine of Discovery established in fifteenth-century papal bulls. These documents authorized Christian nations to claim non-Christian lands and subjugate their inhabitants, creating principles that became Christian international law and were subsequently adopted into American colonial and state law, incorporated into the U.S. Constitution, and affirmed by the Supreme Court in Johnson v. M’Intosh (1823).

John Christopher Pinheiro’s work on the Mexican-American War further substantiates the pattern. Pinheiro documents how “many Americans argued that Roman Catholicism was incompatible with civil and religious liberty” and pointed to “Mexico’s political troubles as an example of this incompatibility in action.” This pre-war framing “helped to establish a preconception of Mexicans as a cognitively inferior, superstitious, and lazy race,” while evangelical Protestants “saw the war as a Providential opportunity to evangelize Catholic Mexico with a ‘pure’ gospel.”

This paper builds upon these historiographical foundations by extracting the underlying pattern: the fusion of elite material ambition with popular apocalyptic narrative. It then asks whether this same pattern can be observed operating at different scales and in different contexts—from the Crusades to contemporary geopolitics.

4. The Crusades and the Medieval Origins of Western Expansion

The paper’s framing of the Crusades as a “template” finds support in specialized scholarship on medieval expansion and its afterlife in later centuries. The crusading ideal proved remarkably adaptable: when Jerusalem was lost, the framework found new targets—heretics in southern France, pagans in the Baltic, political opponents of the papacy in Italy.

This adaptability is crucial for the pattern-based view. The crusade was not a single event but a meme complex—a cluster of ideas (holy war, remission of sins, cosmic struggle) that could attach itself to diverse material projects. The same structure that sent knights to Jerusalem could later send conquistadors to the Americas and settlers across the continent.

This paper draws on crusade historiography not for new archival discoveries but for the pattern it reveals: a religious framework flexible enough to legitimate diverse projects of expansion while providing participants with cosmic meaning.

5. The Frankfurt School and the Critique of Enlightenment

Your bibliography includes Adorno and Horkheimer’s Dialectic of Enlightenment, and this philosophical tradition is central to the paper’s deeper argument. The Frankfurt School theorists argued that Enlightenment reason, intended to liberate humanity from myth, had itself become a new form of mythology—a tool of domination rather than liberation.

This framework directly parallels the paper’s argument about the fusion of material interest and cosmic meaning. For Adorno and Horkheimer, the Enlightenment’s promise of freedom through reason was betrayed when reason became instrumental—a tool for controlling nature and other humans rather than for understanding. The result was a new form of irrationalism disguised as rationality.

This paper extends this critique by showing how the fusion operates not only in Enlightenment philosophy but in the concrete historical projects of Western expansion. The “dialectic of enlightenment” is not an abstract philosophical problem but a lived historical pattern visible in the Crusades, colonialism, Manifest Destiny, and modern interventions.

6. Political Psychology and the Contagion of Leadership

Your extension of the framework—that a psychopathic leader will induce psychopathic values and behaviors in a population—engages a growing literature in political psychology and social contagion theory. This is where the pattern-based view becomes truly powerful: it suggests that the same dynamics that operate at the scale of centuries also operate at the scale of decades, years, and even individual lives.

The literature on authoritarianism has long documented how populations can internalize the values of authoritarian leaders. Theodor Adorno’s The Authoritarian Personality (1950) was an early attempt to understand how psychological dispositions make populations susceptible to fascist appeals. More recent work has examined how leaders shape not only policies but the moral and emotional climate of entire societies.

The pattern-based view suggests that Nazism is better explained through this lens than through exceptionalist accounts that treat it as a unique historical aberration. The fusion of elite ambition (industrialists, military officers, party officials) with popular apocalyptic narrative (the myth of Aryan destiny, the cosmic struggle against Bolshevism and international Jewry) follows precisely the same structure as the Crusades, colonialism, and Manifest Destiny.

This is not to minimize the specific horrors of the Holocaust, nor to suggest that all expansions are morally equivalent. It is to recognize that the underlying pattern—the engine of power fused with meaning—operates across contexts. When a psychopathic leader ascends to power, they do not merely impose their pathology on a passive population. They activate existing cultural narratives, channel material anxieties, and provide a framework that makes psychopathic behavior seem not only acceptable but righteous.

7. Network Theory and Pattern Recognition Across Scales

The pattern-based view finds methodological support in contemporary network theory and complexity science. Recent work on information cascades and social contagion examines how beliefs and behaviors propagate through populations, and how the structure of social networks shapes what spreads.

The key insight for our purposes is that patterns can repeat across scales without requiring direct historical causation. The Crusades did not cause Manifest Destiny. But the same underlying dynamics—elite ambition seeking legitimation, populations seeking meaning, the fusion of the two into an engine of expansion—can operate independently in different times and places because they reflect fundamental features of human social organization.

This is what the paper means by “patterns who can repeat and appear at many scales.” It is not claiming that later expansions were consciously modeled on earlier ones, though sometimes they were. It is claiming that the same structural dynamics tend to produce similar outcomes across different historical contexts.

This paper draws on network theory not for mathematical modeling but for its conceptual vocabulary: cascades, contagion, scale invariance, emergent behavior. These concepts provide a language for describing how patterns propagate and repeat without requiring conspiracy or direct influence.

8. The Taboo Zone: Pattern Recognition as Suspect

We must acknowledge, as with the previous papers, the academic taboos this work challenges. Pattern-based historical analysis occupies an uncomfortable position in the academy. It risks being accused of:

  • Reductionism: collapsing complex historical events into a single explanatory framework.
  • Conspiracy theory: suggesting hidden connections across time and space.
  • Presentism: reading contemporary concerns into the past.
  • Moral equivalency: comparing events that are not morally comparable.

These risks are real. The paper must navigate them carefully. But the taboo against pattern recognition also has costs. It can prevent us from seeing genuine structural continuities that operate across centuries. It can trap us in exceptionalist narratives that treat each historical event as sui generis, obscuring the dynamics they share.

Jiang Xueqin’s work has faced similar critiques. Some commentators have questioned whether his predictions “rely on selective historical analogies, speculative game-theoretic reasoning, and untestable assumptions”. Yet the accuracy of two of his three major 2024 predictions has forced a reconsideration of his methodology. The pattern-based approach, when applied rigorously, can generate testable claims about the future—a powerful argument for its validity.

This paper accepts these risks because the patterns it identifies are too persistent and too consequential to ignore. The fusion of elite ambition and popular apocalyptic narrative has shaped Western expansion for a millennium. To refuse to see it because seeing it is uncomfortable is to remain willfully blind.

Introduction: An Invitation to Look Anew

Every society tells itself a story about its past. These stories are not false, but they are inevitably partial. They emphasize certain events and downplay others; they highlight noble intentions and sometimes obscure inconvenient consequences. This is not malice. It is how collective memory functions. It provides cohesion, meaning, and a sense of moral direction.

This paper is an invitation to consider an additional layer to the Western story. It is not an attempt to replace the existing narrative, but to supplement it with a lens that reveals certain recurring patterns—patterns that connect the Crusader marching toward Jerusalem, the Conquistador sailing for the New World, the pioneer venturing west under Manifest Destiny, the Nazi marching under the swastika, and the modern soldier deployed in the name of spreading freedom.

The lens is this: Western expansion has been consistently driven by a fusion between the material ambitions of powerful groups and the apocalyptic or quasi-religious yearnings of broader populations. These two forces, the cynical and the sincere, have repeatedly converged to create an engine of enormous power—one capable of justifying immense violence, sustaining itself across centuries, and adapting its language to suit each new era.

To examine this is not to accuse every participant of bad faith. On the contrary, it is to take sincere belief seriously, and to ask the difficult question: how does sincere belief become entangled with systems of power that it would otherwise abhor? And what might it mean to recognize this entanglement?

Part I: The Engine Defined

Before tracing the engine through history, we must understand its components.

The First Component: Pragmatic Ambition

Every society has its elites—individuals and groups who accumulate disproportionate influence over resources, institutions, and decision-making. Their motivations are generally not mysterious. They seek to preserve and expand their position. This can mean securing access to valuable trade routes, acquiring land and resources, establishing military advantage, or controlling markets. In the medieval period, this meant popes seeking authority over kings and Italian city-states seeking control of Mediterranean trade. In the colonial era, it meant monarchs filling treasuries and companies seeking sugar and cotton. In the modern era, it means securing oil, maintaining global military basing, and ensuring favorable conditions for multinational corporations.

This component is not inherently evil. It is a description of how power tends to operate. But it is incomplete without the second component.

The Second Component: The Search for Cosmic Meaning

Human beings are not merely economic actors. We are meaning-makers. We crave narratives that place our lives within a larger story, that give our struggles purpose and our sacrifices significance. The most powerful of these narratives have often been religious, but they can also take secularized forms: the mission to spread democracy, the duty to defend civilization, the destiny to tame a wilderness, the destiny of a master race.

When these two components operate separately, their effects are limited. Ambitious elites without a compelling narrative can only rule through brute force, which is expensive and unstable. A population with powerful beliefs but no organized power can sustain a movement, but may struggle to shape large-scale events. The danger—and the engine—emerges when the two fuse.

The Fusion

When elite ambition aligns with popular apocalyptic or quasi-religious narrative, each amplifies the other. The elite gain legitimacy, popular enthusiasm, and a motivated workforce or soldiery. The population gains a sense of cosmic purpose, a conviction that their actions serve not just material interests but divine or moral destiny. The fusion produces a force capable of immense destruction, precisely because it combines the cold calculation of power with the white-hot energy of faith.

Crucially, this fusion does not require conscious conspiracy. It often emerges organically, as elites learn to speak the language of the people, and as religious leaders interpret events in ways that align with the interests of the powerful. The result is a system that perpetuates itself without anyone needing to be fully aware of its mechanics.

The Deep Temporal Context: Steppe Invasions and European Formation

To understand why European societies developed this particular engine of expansion, we must look deeper—to the millennia of steppe invasions that shaped the continent’s genetic, cultural, and political landscape. From the Scythians in the first millennium BCE to the Huns in the fourth and fifth centuries, the Avars and Magyars in subsequent centuries, and the Mongol invasions of the thirteenth century, Europe was repeatedly subjected to waves of mounted warriors from the Eurasian steppe.

This continuous pressure had several lasting effects:

  • Militarization: European societies developed fortified structures and military technologies (the castle, heavy cavalry, eventually gunpowder) partially in response to steppe threats.
  • Cultural memory: The figure of the “barbarian” from the East became embedded in European consciousness, later to be projected onto other peoples during expansion.
  • Release valve dynamics: When internal pressures built up—surplus warrior populations, religious fervor, economic ambition—the memory of invasion from the East could be inverted into a mandate for invasion toward the East (the Crusades) or, when the Atlantic opened, toward the West.

The steppe hypothesis suggests that the engine this paper describes did not emerge from a vacuum. It was forged in response to millennia of pressure from the East—a pressure that shaped European societies in ways that predisposed them toward expansion when conditions allowed.

Part II: The Prototype—The Crusades as Template

The Crusades provide our clearest view of this engine in its formative stage.

Consider the situation in late 11th-century Europe. The papal reform movement had strengthened the papacy, but it still struggled for authority against secular rulers and the Eastern Church. The feudal system produced a class of younger sons of nobles with no land and no prospects. Italian maritime republics like Venice and Genoa sought advantage in Mediterranean trade. Into this environment came Pope Urban II’s call at Clermont in 1095.

The stated purpose was religious: to liberate Jerusalem from Muslim rule and to aid Eastern Christians. The promise was apocalyptic: participants would receive remission of sins, a spiritual reward that transcended earthly concerns. The enemy was framed as cosmic: not merely political opponents, but defilers of holy places, enemies of God.

Yet the material outcomes were unmistakable. The Crusades extended papal influence. They provided an outlet for restless warriors, channeling their violence outward rather than inward. They opened trade routes that enriched European merchants. They established Crusader states that functioned as new feudal domains for ambitious nobles.

Were the Crusaders merely cynical adventurers using religion as a cover? Some may have been. But the historical record suggests that most participants were sincere in their faith. They believed they were undertaking a holy mission. The tragedy—and the pattern—is that their sincere belief served interests far beyond their own salvation.

The Crusades also reveal the engine’s adaptability. When Jerusalem was lost, the framework did not collapse. It simply found new targets: heretics in southern France, pagans in the Baltic, political opponents of the papacy in Italy. The crusading ideal became a flexible meme, capable of legitimating diverse projects of expansion and domination.

Part III: The Expansion—Colonialism and the New World

When European powers turned their attention across the Atlantic, the crusading framework traveled with them, though its language evolved.

The material ambitions are well-documented. Spain sought gold and silver. Portugal sought a route to the spice trade. England and France sought territory and resources. Joint-stock companies emerged to finance and profit from colonial ventures. These were not hidden motives; they were openly declared.

Yet the enterprise could not have succeeded on greed alone. It required a legitimating narrative. This came from the same fusion we have observed.

The Doctrine of Discovery, articulated in papal bulls of the 15th century—Pope Nicholas V’s Romanus Pontifex (1455) and Pope Alexander VI’s Inter Caetera (1493)—provided the theological framework: Christian nations had the right to claim lands not ruled by Christians, and to subjugate their inhabitants for the purpose of conversion. These documents established what became Christian international law, creating principles that would shape European colonization for centuries.

The Puritan colonies of New England offer a particularly pure expression of the fusion. John Winthrop’s vision of a “City upon a Hill” explicitly invoked biblical imagery. The colonists saw themselves as a new Israel, fleeing bondage in Europe to establish a holy commonwealth in the wilderness. Their expansion was not conquest but fulfillment of covenant. The native inhabitants were not nations with their own rights and histories, but either potential converts or demonic impediments to be cleared away.

The same framework justified the enslavement of Africans. A twisted theology developed: Africans were descendants of Ham, cursed to servitude; their enslavement, followed by forced conversion, was presented as rescuing them from paganism. The material interests of plantation owners and slave traders fused with a narrative that gave cosmic meaning to atrocity.

Part IV: The Culmination—Manifest Destiny and the Nineteenth Century

The 19th century gave this fusion its most explicit American expression: Manifest Destiny.

The phrase itself, coined in 1845, captured both components perfectly. “Manifest” suggested obviousness, self-evidence, truth that required no argument. “Destiny” suggested inevitability, divine ordination, a future already written. The expansion of the United States across the continent was framed not as conquest but as fulfillment.

The Legal Foundation: As Robert J. Miller’s scholarship demonstrates, Manifest Destiny arose directly from the legal principles of the Doctrine of Discovery. This international law was adopted into American colonial and state law, incorporated into the U.S. Constitution, and affirmed by the Supreme Court in Johnson v. M’Intosh (1823). The framework was not merely rhetorical; it was embedded in American law.

The Religious Dimension: John Christopher Pinheiro’s research on the Mexican-American War reveals how anti-Catholicism and crusading language shaped American perceptions. Many Americans argued that “Roman Catholicism was incompatible with civil and religious liberty” and pointed to “Mexico’s political troubles as an example of this incompatibility in action.” Evangelical Protestants “saw the war as a Providential opportunity to evangelize Catholic Mexico with a ‘pure’ gospel.”

The cost to indigenous peoples was catastrophic. Yet it was framed within the same narrative. Native Americans were either obstacles to progress or people to be “civilized.” The Indian Removal Act was presented as humanitarian—separating native peoples from the corrupting influence of white settlement. The Trail of Tears was justified as necessary for the greater good.

Part V: The Pathology—Nazism as Pattern

The pattern-based view finds one of its clearest expressions in Nazi Germany. Here, the fusion of elite ambition and popular apocalyptic narrative operated with terrifying efficiency.

The Material Component: German industrialists, military officers, and party officials had concrete material interests: rearmament, territorial expansion, access to resources, elimination of political opposition. The SS became an economic empire, operating businesses and exploiting slave labor. These were not irrational actors pursuing abstract ideology; they were rational actors pursuing power and profit.

The Narrative Component: The Nazi movement offered a cosmic narrative: the Aryan race as the bearer of civilization, locked in an apocalyptic struggle with Jewish Bolshevism, destined to expand into living space in the East. This was not merely propaganda for mass consumption. Leading Nazis sincerely believed elements of this mythology. Himmler’s SS was organized as a quasi-religious order, complete with rituals, ceremonies, and an eschatological vision.

The Fusion: Elite interests and popular belief amplified each other. Industrialists profited from rearmament while believing they were serving the nation. Soldiers marched east convinced they were defending civilization against subhuman enemies. Ordinary Germans participated in atrocities while believing they were fulfilling their duty to a higher cause.

The Psychopathic Leader Hypothesis: Adolf Hitler exhibited classic psychopathic traits: lack of empathy, grandiosity, manipulativeness, pathological lying. But the horror of Nazism cannot be explained by Hitler’s psychology alone. The fusion occurred because Hitler’s pathology resonated with existing cultural narratives (German nationalism, antisemitism, anti-communism) and material conditions (economic crisis, resentment of Versailles). The population did not become clinically psychopathic, but they adopted psychopathic values—the devaluation of outgroups, the suspension of moral constraints, the willingness to commit atrocities.

This is the pattern at work. Nazism was not a unique historical aberration. It was an expression of the same engine that drove the Crusades, colonialism, and Manifest Destiny—adapted to German cultural materials and twentieth-century conditions.

Part VI: The Adaptation—The Modern Era

After World War II, explicit religious language became less acceptable in formal statecraft. Yet the underlying structure of the fusion adapted, finding new language for old patterns.

The Cold War provided the first great modern framework. The enemy was communism, framed not merely as a political opponent but as an “evil empire,” an atheistic force threatening civilization itself. The language was secularized—“freedom” and “democracy” replaced “Christendom” and “the faith”—but the structure was identical. The West had a cosmic mission: to defend the free world, to contain evil, to ultimately triumph in a struggle of light against darkness.

The material interests were substantial. The military-industrial complex grew enormously. Defense contracts flowed to corporations. Strategic bases were established around the world. Access to oil in the Middle East became a paramount concern.

The War on Terror, launched after September 11, 2001, represents the purest modern expression of the fusion. The enemy was not a state but an abstract evil: “terror” itself. This framing allowed for endless war, as terror could never be fully defeated. It justified interventions across the globe, suspension of civil liberties at home, and a permanent state of emergency.

The language was explicitly Manichaean. President George W. Bush spoke of an “axis of evil.” He framed the conflict as a struggle between good and evil, between civilization and barbarism. “Either you are with us, or you are with the terrorists.” This was the language of crusade, updated for a secular age.

Yet the material outcomes were concrete. The Iraq War, justified through claims about weapons of mass destruction that proved false, secured American military presence in an oil-rich region and awarded lucrative contracts to defense and reconstruction firms.

Part VII: The Contemporary Edge—Israel and Eschatology

Nowhere is this fusion more visible today than in the intersection of American foreign policy toward Israel and the eschatological beliefs of some Christian fundamentalists.

The material and strategic interests are clear. Israel has long been viewed by American strategists as a crucial ally in a volatile region. It provides intelligence cooperation, military basing access, and a counterweight to hostile regional powers.

Yet this alliance also receives fervent support from a different quarter: evangelical Christians who believe that the gathering of Jews in the Holy Land is a necessary precondition for the End Times and the Second Coming of Christ. For these believers, support for Israel is not a matter of geopolitics but of prophecy. It is a sacred duty.

Recent scholarship documents this fusion. Motti Inbari and Kirill Bumin’s research on Christian Zionism finds “significant theological and eschatological differences among evangelical pastors which are likely to have direct implications on their current foreign policy views, and especially on their support for the State of Israel.” Stephen Sizer’s work identifies “seven basic theological tenets that distinguish the various strands within the Christian Zionist movement,” including beliefs that have direct “political consequences.”

The convergence is striking. Strategists and believers find themselves advocating for the same policies, for entirely different reasons. Each reinforces the other. The believers provide grassroots energy and voting power. The strategists provide access and influence. The result is a policy that can survive even when it conflicts with other American interests or values.

Part VIII: Seeing Gently—Compassion and Complexity

At this point, a reader might feel uncomfortable. The framework can seem to reduce history to a sordid tale of manipulation and delusion. It can feel like an attack on sincere faith, on genuine patriotism, on the noble intentions of countless individuals who have sought to do good.

This discomfort must be taken seriously. If the lens cannot accommodate compassion, it is not a lens but a weapon.

So let us be clear about what this framework does not claim.

It does not claim that every participant in these historical processes was cynical or malevolent. Most were not. The Crusader who marched east, the colonist who crossed the Atlantic, the pioneer who ventured west, the German soldier who fought for the Reich, the American soldier who served in Iraq—these were ordinary human beings, seeking meaning, security, and purpose. They loved their families, mourned their dead, and tried, in their own way, to live good lives.

It does not claim that religious or moral belief is merely a mask for power. Belief is real. It shapes action. It gives life meaning. To suggest otherwise is to misunderstand what it means to be human.

It does not claim that all outcomes of Western expansion have been negative. The tradition that produced the Crusades also produced universities, hospitals, and works of art. The civilization that engaged in colonialism also developed concepts of human rights, democracy, and universal dignity. We are not required to choose between celebration and condemnation.

What the framework does claim is this: Sincere belief and material power have repeatedly become entangled in ways that serve the interests of the powerful while providing cosmic meaning to the population, and this entanglement has produced consequences that neither group fully intended or fully perceived.

The tragedy is not that people believe. The tragedy is that their belief can be woven into systems of violence and exploitation without their knowledge or consent.

Part IX: The Gentle Purpose—Seeing as Liberation

If this framework is not about blame, what is its purpose? Why offer this lens at all?

The answer is that seeing clearly is itself a form of liberation. Systems of power maintain themselves partly by becoming invisible. They become the water in which we swim, the air we breathe. We do not question them because we do not see them. The fusion of elite interest and popular narrative is most powerful when it is most hidden.

To see the pattern is to begin to question. Not to reject everything, but to ask: In what ways might my sincere beliefs be serving purposes I have not considered? In what ways might the narratives I absorb from my culture be channeling my energy toward ends I would not endorse if I saw them clearly? In what ways might I be participating in systems that I would oppose if I understood them?

These are not comfortable questions. But they are the questions of maturity. A child accepts the stories they are told. An adult learns to examine them. A society that cannot examine its own stories is a society that cannot grow.

The gentle purpose of this lens is to invite that examination. Not to force it, not to shame those who decline it, but to offer it as a tool for those who seek greater clarity.

Jiang Xueqin’s work demonstrates that this kind of seeing has practical value. By recognizing patterns, we can anticipate outcomes. His framework, which combines “recurring historical patterns, geopolitical incentives, and game theory,” has generated predictions that have proven remarkably accurate. This is not prophecy; it is structural analysis applied rigorously.

Conclusion: The Continuous Crusade and the Possibility of Awakening

We have traced a pattern across a millennium. From the Crusader marching to Jerusalem, to the Conquistador sailing for the New World, to the Puritan building a city on a hill, to the pioneer fulfilling Manifest Destiny, to the Nazi marching under the swastika, to the modern soldier fighting to spread freedom, to the believer supporting Israel to hasten the End Times—through all these, we have seen the same engine at work.

It is the engine of power fused with meaning, of elite ambition channeling popular faith, of sincere belief serving purposes beyond itself. It is the continuous crusade.

The pattern-based view reveals that these are not isolated phenomena. They are expressions of a single, adaptive structure—one that operates across centuries, across cultures, across scales. The same dynamics that drove the Crusades drove Manifest Destiny and Nazism and the War on Terror. Not because later actors consciously imitated earlier ones, but because the underlying structure of power and meaning tends to produce similar outcomes in similar conditions.

The deep temporal context of steppe invasions reminds us that even this engine has preconditions—historical forces that shaped European societies in ways that made them expansion-prone. The pattern at one scale is nested within patterns at larger scales.

To name it is not to end it. Patterns this deep do not dissolve simply because they are recognized. But recognition is a beginning. It is the moment when we stop being merely carried by the current and begin to see the current itself. It is the moment when we can ask, for the first time: Do I want to go where this current is taking me? Is this really the story I want to be part of?

These questions are not comfortable. They are not easy. But they are the questions of moral awakening. And if the continuous crusade has taught us anything, it is that such awakening is always possible—and always, for those who benefit from the current, deeply threatening.

The purpose of this gentle inquiry has been to make that awakening a little more possible, a little less frightening, and a little more available to all of us who swim in waters we did not create and do not fully see.

An Invitation to Collaboration

This paper has offered a pattern-based lens for re-examining Western expansion from the Crusades to the present. It suggests that the same underlying structure—the fusion of elite ambition with popular apocalyptic narrative—has operated across centuries and scales, producing similar outcomes in different contexts.

The framework remains conceptual. It requires testing, refinement, and application across multiple domains. We invite collaboration from:

  • Historians to test the framework against archival evidence, identifying cases where the fusion operated and cases where it did not.
  • Political scientists to examine how the pattern manifests in contemporary geopolitics and foreign policy.
  • Sociologists to study how apocalyptic narratives spread through populations and how they interact with elite interests.
  • Psychologists to investigate the mechanisms by which populations internalize the values of psychopathic leaders.
  • Network theorists to model how the fusion propagates through social systems and how it might be interrupted.
  • Theologians and religious scholars to bring their understanding of apocalyptic traditions to bear on the pattern’s religious dimensions.
  • Macro-historians and pattern theorists working in the tradition of Jiang Xueqin to refine our understanding of how deep structural patterns shape human events across scales and centuries.

The pattern is real. It matters. It shapes our world in ways we are only beginning to understand. Understanding it better—with rigor, compassion, and intellectual humility—is a task worthy of our best efforts.

The pattern repeats. The engine runs. Seeing is the beginning.

Individualism vs Solidarity vs Communism

An Essay on Socioeconomic Patterns and the Psychopathic Gradient

Prologue: The Map of Possibility

Every society is an answer to a question that has never been satisfactorily resolved: how should human beings live together? The question is ancient, perhaps as ancient as consciousness itself. But the answers have proliferated like species in an ecosystem—each adapted to its environment, each carrying its own costs and benefits, each claiming universality while bearing the marks of its particular origins.

This essay examines three such answers: individualism, solidarity, and communism. They are not the only possibilities, but they represent three fundamental orientations toward the problem of collective life. Individualism places the sovereign self at the center. Communism places the collective whole at the center. Solidarity places the relationship between selves at the center.

The argument advanced here is that these are not merely different political philosophies but different energy configurations—different ways of organizing human energy, material resources, and meaning-making. Each requires a characteristic level of energy to maintain. Each tends toward a characteristic equilibrium. And each, when examined closely, reveals itself to be not an eternal verity but a contingent solution to specific historical conditions.

The deepest claim of this essay is that solidarity is the lowest energy state—the configuration toward which human societies naturally tend when external pressures are removed. Individualism and communism are higher-energy configurations, requiring continuous input to maintain their specific topologies. They are, in thermodynamic terms, metastable: they can persist for long periods, but only through constant work against the gradient.

This essay introduces an additional dimension: the psychopathic gradient. It proposes that societies, like individuals, can be arrayed along a spectrum of psychopathy—from those organized around empathy, reciprocity, and mutual recognition to those organized around exploitation, manipulation, and the instrumental treatment of others. Individualism and communism, as high-energy configurations, tend to select for psychopathic traits in their elites and to normalize psychopathic behavior in their populations through a process this essay terms socioeconomic patterns echoing.

This is not a moral claim about the people within these systems. Most individuals in individualist societies are not psychopaths. Most individuals in communist societies are not psychopaths. The claim is about the logic of the system—the traits it rewards, the behaviors it normalizes, the character it selects for in those who rise to its heights.

The evidence for these claims is everywhere and nowhere—everywhere in the spontaneous emergence of mutual aid in crisis, in the persistent human capacity for recognition across boundaries, in the Christmas Truce of 1914; and nowhere in our formal histories, which document the machines so thoroughly that we have forgotten the ground on which they stand.

This essay attempts to make the invisible visible: to map the energy landscape of human society, to trace the psychopathic gradient across that landscape, and to locate three great experiments upon that map.

Part One: The Individual

The Sovereign Self

Individualism begins with a proposition that seems self-evident to those raised within its horizon: that the individual is the fundamental unit of social reality. Groups are aggregates of individuals. Institutions are instruments of individuals. Rights belong to individuals. Value is created by individuals. Freedom means the absence of external constraint on individual choice.

This orientation has deep roots in Western thought. Its philosophical genealogy runs through Locke’s natural rights, Smith’s economic man, Mill’s liberty principle, and the entire tradition of classical liberalism. Its political expression is the constitutional order that protects individual rights against collective encroachment. Its economic expression is the market, where individuals pursue their interests through voluntary exchange. Its cultural expression is the ideal of authenticity—the obligation to discover and express one’s unique self.

The individualist vision is not without grandeur. It has inspired movements of liberation from feudal bondage, from absolutist states, from conformist pressures. It has fueled extraordinary creativity, innovation, and prosperity. It has given us the novel, the symphony, the scientific revolution, and the declaration that all humans are created equal.

But individualism, as a complete social philosophy, rests on a series of assumptions that are not obviously true.

The Assumptions of Individualism

The assumption of autonomy. Individualism assumes that persons are, in some fundamental sense, self-originating—that we arrive in the world as bounded units capable of independent existence. But this is false. No human being survives infancy without sustained care. No human being develops language, culture, or identity without immersion in a pre-existing social world. We are, from the beginning, fundamentally dependent and fundamentally relational.

The assumption of self-sufficiency. Individualism assumes that adults can and should be self-sufficient—that dependence is a failure to be overcome. But this is also false. Throughout our lives, we depend on others for material needs, emotional support, cognitive development, and meaning. Even the most “independent” among us relies on vast networks of cooperation—farmers who grow food, workers who maintain infrastructure, strangers who follow rules that make social life possible.

The assumption of atomism. Individualism treats society as an aggregate of pre-formed individuals who choose to associate for mutual benefit. But this gets the direction of causation backward. We do not first exist as individuals and then enter society. We are constituted as individuals through society—through the relationships, languages, and institutions that shape us from before birth.

The assumption of scarcity. Individualism typically assumes a world of scarce resources in which individuals must compete for survival and flourishing. This assumption becomes self-fulfilling: when everyone acts as a self-interested atom, the world does become a competitive arena. But it is not obviously the only possible world. Abundance can be created through cooperation. Needs can be met through sharing. The scarcity assumption may tell us more about individualist culture than about the human condition.

The Costs of Individualism

Individualism, pursued as a complete social philosophy, generates characteristic pathologies.

Anomie. When individuals are cut loose from stable social bonds and expected to construct meaning from their own resources, many fail. The result is loneliness, meaninglessness, and the desperate pursuit of substitutes—consumption, status, stimulation. Durkheim documented this a century ago; the data on loneliness and mental health crisis confirms it today.

Exploitation. In a society of self-interested individuals, those with greater resources, power, or strategic advantage can exploit those with less. The market, left to itself, generates massive inequality. The strong dominate the weak. The rhetoric of freedom becomes a justification for predation.

Erosion of care. Care work—raising children, tending the sick, supporting the frail, maintaining relationships—is systematically devalued in individualist societies because it produces diffuse, long-term, non-monetizable benefits. Those who do this work (disproportionately women) are economically disadvantaged. The work itself becomes invisible.

Ecological destruction. Individualist societies organized around consumption and growth treat the natural world as a collection of resources to be exploited. The long-term consequences—climate change, biodiversity loss, resource depletion—are borne by everyone, but no individual has an incentive to restrain their own consumption. The result is collective suicide through individual rationality.

The paradox of freedom. Individualism promises freedom but delivers a peculiar form of unfreedom: the compulsion to choose, to compete, to perform, to succeed. The autonomous self must constantly construct itself, market itself, improve itself. There is no rest, no givenness, no belonging. Freedom becomes a burden.

Part Two: The Psychopathic Gradient

The Trait Cluster

Before examining how individualism and communism relate to psychopathy, we must understand what psychopathy is. Clinical psychology identifies a cluster of traits that define the condition:

Domain Traits
Interpersonal Grandiose sense of self-worth, pathological lying, manipulativeness, glibness/superficial charm
Affective Lack of empathy, shallow affect, callous lack of remorse, failure to accept responsibility
Lifestyle Parasitic orientation, lack of realistic long-term goals, impulsivity, irresponsibility
Antisocial Poor behavioral controls, early behavioral problems, criminal versatility

Psychopathy exists on a spectrum. Most people possess some of these traits to some degree. The clinical diagnosis is reserved for those at the extreme end. But the traits themselves are distributed throughout the population.

Socioeconomic Patterns Echoing

This essay introduces a concept: socioeconomic patterns echoing. It proposes that the values and behaviors of a society’s elite do not remain confined to that elite. They echo downward and outward, shaping the norms, institutions, and expectations of the entire society.

The mechanism operates through multiple channels:

  • Media representation: The behaviors of the powerful are portrayed as admirable, aspirational, or at least normal. The billionaire’s ruthlessness becomes “business acumen.” The politician’s manipulation becomes “political skill.” The executive’s lack of empathy becomes “tough decision-making.”

  • Institutional design: Institutions are structured to reward traits that elites possess. Schools teach competition. Corporations reward those who prioritize profit over people. Legal systems protect those who can afford the best manipulation.

  • Cultural narratives: Stories celebrate the psychopathic traits reframed as virtues. The “self-made man” who tramples others on the way up. The “disruptor” who destroys communities in the name of innovation. The “winner” who plays to win, regardless of cost.

  • Normalization through repetition: Over time, behaviors that were once shocking become ordinary. The threshold for what is acceptable shifts. Each generation inherits a world in which the psychopathic traits are more normal than they were for the previous generation.

  • Self-selection of elites: As the culture normalizes psychopathic traits, those who possess them most abundantly rise most easily. They are selected for by the very institutions their predecessors shaped. The elite becomes more psychopathic over time, which accelerates the echoing.

This is a recursive process. The elite shapes the culture. The culture selects the elite. The echo amplifies with each cycle.

Individualism and the Psychopathic Profile

If we examine the behavioral profile of clinical psychopathy alongside the idealized individualist, the parallels are striking:

Psychopathy (clinical criteria) Individualism (idealized form)
Lack of empathy Emotional self-reliance; not being “needy”
Grandiose sense of self-worth Healthy self-esteem; belief in one’s own merit
Pathological lying Strategic self-presentation; privacy; not oversharing
Manipulativeness Negotiation skills; getting the best deal
Shallow affect Stoicism; emotional control
Callous lack of remorse Not looking back; not being weighed down by guilt
Failure to accept responsibility Taking credit for successes; not dwelling on failures
Parasitic lifestyle Entrepreneurship; living off one’s wits
Poor behavioral controls Disciplined pursuit of self-interest
Lack of realistic long-term goals Flexibility; adapting to opportunities
Impulsivity Decisiveness; seizing the moment
Irresponsibility Not being beholden to others’ expectations

The correspondence is not perfect, but it is deeply suggestive. The individualist virtues, when described by their advocates, sound remarkably like the psychopathic traits, when described by clinicians—only with the valence reversed. What the clinician calls pathology, the individualist calls strength.

This is the valence reversal at the heart of socioeconomic patterns echoing. Individualism takes traits that would be recognized as pathological in any close relationship and reframes them as virtues in the public sphere. The same behaviors are judged differently depending on context—and individualism works to expand the context in which the psychopathic judgment applies.

The Normalization Process in Individualist Societies

How does this normalization occur?

Step 1: Abstraction. Individualism abstracts the person from their relationships. The “individual” of liberal theory is not a father, a neighbor, a friend, a community member. He is a bare unit of choice and preference, stripped of all the particular bonds that make him who he is. In this abstraction, the absence of empathy is not lack; it is simply not relevant.

Step 2: Legitimation. Individualism provides narratives that make psychopathic behavior seem noble. The “rugged individualist” who stands alone against the crowd. The “disruptor” who breaks traditions without sentiment. The “self-made man” who owes nothing to anyone. These narratives transform pathology into cultural heroism.

Step 3: Institutionalization. Individualism creates institutions that reward psychopathic traits and punish their opposites. The market rewards those who can ignore the human consequences of their decisions. The legal system protects those who act in their self-interest within the rules. The culture celebrates those who “win” without regard for how they win.

Step 4: Normalization. Over time, the abnormal becomes normal. Children are raised to be “independent,” which means not needing others. Schools teach “critical thinking,” which often means skepticism toward inherited bonds. Media celebrates “success,” which means accumulation regardless of cost. The psychopathic traits become aspirational.

Step 5: Pathologizing the alternative. Those who maintain the old virtues—loyalty, reciprocity, care, dependence—are pathologized in turn. They are “codependent,” “enmeshed,” “needy,” “unable to set boundaries.” The language of therapy, ironically, is used to undermine the very relationships that make therapy unnecessary.

Self-Selection of Psychopathic Elites

As this process advances, those who rise to the top are increasingly those who most perfectly embody the psychopathic traits that the culture now celebrates.

The corporate executive who can lay off thousands without losing sleep. The politician who can say anything to win. The financier who can extract wealth without creating value. The media mogul who can manipulate public opinion for profit. These are not exceptions to individualism. They are its ideal types—the ones at whom the system points and says: “See? This is what success looks like. This is what you should aspire to be.”

And because they are at the top, their behavior echoes downward. Their ruthlessness becomes the benchmark for ambition. Their manipulation becomes the model for negotiation. Their lack of empathy becomes the standard for professionalism.

The echo amplifies. The elite becomes more psychopathic. The culture becomes more psychopathic. The elite becomes more psychopathic still.

The Communist Variant

Communism, in theory, offers the opposite: the dissolution of the individual into the collective. But actually existing communism produces its own relationship to psychopathy.

The communist elite exhibits a different but equally disturbing profile:

Communist Elite Behavior Underlying Trait
Purges of rivals Callous lack of remorse
Cult of personality Grandiose sense of self-worth
Show trials Pathological lying
Surveillance state Manipulativeness
Forced collectivization Lack of empathy
Rewriting history Failure to accept responsibility
Privilege within equality Parasitic orientation

Communism does not eliminate psychopathy. It channels it through different institutions. The party loyalist who denounces others to save himself. The bureaucrat who extracts privilege from the plan. The informer who betrays neighbors for state favor. These are not expressions of solidarity. They are psychopathy in collectivist drag.

And the same echoing process occurs. The party elite’s behavior becomes the model for aspiring party members. The culture of denunciation spreads. The suspicion, the manipulation, the instrumental treatment of others—these echo downward until they saturate society.

The difference is that communism’s psychopathy is collectivist rather than individualist. It operates through the party rather than the market. But it is psychopathy nonetheless.

Solidarity as Psychopathy-Suppressing

Solidarity offers a different relationship to psychopathy. In a solidarity-based community:

  • Psychopathic behavior is quickly recognized because people know each other
  • Exploitation is costly because cheaters cannot simply move on
  • The community can protect itself through exclusion without creating a state
  • Children are raised in relationship, which is the best protection against psychopathy
  • The culture values the traits that psychopaths lack: empathy, reciprocity, commitment
  • Those who consistently harm others are not celebrated but excluded

This is not a guarantee. No system is immune to pathology. But solidarity creates conditions in which psychopathy is costly rather than rewarding. It tips the evolutionary calculus back toward cooperation. It breaks the echo.

The Christmas Truce is solidarity in action. It is the moment when the fog of psychopathic normalization lifts and human beings recognize each other. The authorities’ response—the suppression, the punishment, the censorship—is the echo fighting back, trying to restore the conditions in which psychopathy can flourish.

Part Three: The Collective

The Sovereign Whole

Communism begins with a proposition that seems self-evident to those raised within its horizon: that the collective is the fundamental unit of social reality. Individuals are products of their social conditions. Freedom means participation in collective self-determination. Value is created by social labor. Justice means the abolition of class distinctions and the common ownership of the means of production.

This orientation also has deep roots in Western thought. Its philosophical genealogy runs through Plato’s republic, More’s utopia, Rousseau’s general will, Marx’s historical materialism, and the entire tradition of socialist and communist thought. Its political expression is the revolutionary party that seizes state power in the name of the proletariat. Its economic expression is central planning and collective ownership. Its cultural expression is the subordination of individual expression to collective purposes.

The communist vision is not without grandeur. It has inspired movements against exploitation, imperialism, and inequality. It has fueled extraordinary solidarity in struggle, remarkable feats of collective construction, and the dream of a world without class. It has given us the Paris Commune, the Russian Revolution, the Chinese Revolution, and the declaration that the free development of each is the condition for the free development of all.

But communism, as a complete social philosophy, rests on assumptions that are not obviously true.

The Assumptions of Communism

The assumption of class primacy. Communism assumes that class is the fundamental social division and that its abolition will resolve other forms of oppression. But history suggests otherwise. Gender, race, ethnicity, religion, and other forms of identity have proven remarkably persistent, generating oppression and conflict even in societies that have abolished private property.

The assumption of revolutionary transformation. Communism assumes that a revolutionary seizure of power can fundamentally remake society. But the experience of actually existing communism suggests that revolutionary seizures tend to produce new elites, new hierarchies, and new forms of domination—often more oppressive than the old.

The assumption of central planning. Communism assumes that a central authority can rationally coordinate economic activity to meet human needs. But the experience of Soviet-style planning revealed massive inefficiencies, information problems, and incentive failures. The economy was too complex to be planned from the center; the result was shortage, waste, and black markets.

The assumption of human malleability. Communism assumes that human nature can be fundamentally transformed through changed social conditions. But human beings have proven more stubborn than theory predicted. The “new Soviet man” never arrived. Old patterns of competition, status-seeking, and corruption re-emerged in new forms.

The assumption of vanguard knowledge. Communism assumes that some—the party, the theorists, the vanguard—can know what is best for all. This assumption justifies enormous concentrations of power and the suppression of dissent. It treats ordinary people as objects of transformation rather than subjects of their own lives.

The Costs of Communism

Communism, pursued as a complete social philosophy, generates characteristic pathologies.

Authoritarianism. The attempt to remake society from above requires enormous concentrations of power. Dissent must be suppressed. Alternatives must be eliminated. The result is not liberation but a new form of unfreedom—often more total than the old because it claims to speak in the name of liberation itself.

Bureaucratic ossification. Central planning creates vast bureaucracies that develop their own interests and logics. The plan becomes an end in itself. Meeting targets replaces meeting needs. Innovation is stifled because it disrupts the plan. The system becomes rigid, unresponsive, and incapable of learning.

The elimination of difference. Communism’s universalist vision tends to suppress local, cultural, and individual particularity. Everyone must be the same, think the same, want the same. The result is cultural destruction, forced assimilation, and the loss of the diverse ways of being human that have evolved over millennia.

The betrayal of solidarity. Communism claims to be the highest expression of human solidarity. But in practice, it has often destroyed the organic solidarities of family, community, and tradition—replacing them with the abstract solidarity of party and state, which is no solidarity at all but a new form of control.

The paradox of liberation. Communism promises liberation from exploitation but delivers a peculiar form of unfreedom: the compulsion to serve the collective, to subordinate oneself to the plan, to be remade according to a blueprint. The individual disappears into the mass. There is no space for the personal, the idiosyncratic, the unpredictable.

Part Four: The Relation

The Sovereign Between

Solidarity begins with a different proposition: that the relationship between persons is the fundamental social reality. Neither the individual nor the collective is primary. What is primary is the space between—the bonds of recognition, reciprocity, and mutual commitment that connect human beings to one another.

This orientation has roots in many traditions. It is present in Indigenous philosophies that understand the self as constituted through relationships to human and more-than-human kin. It is present in religious ethics of love and compassion. It is present in anarchist thought that values voluntary association over state or market. It is present in feminist ethics of care that center the work of maintaining relationships. It is present in mutual aid practices that have sustained communities across time and place.

Solidarity is not an “-ism” in the same sense as individualism or communism. It is not a system to be imposed or a theory to be believed. It is a practice—a way of being in relation to others. It emerges from below rather than being imposed from above. It is maintained through direct interaction rather than institutional enforcement.

The Principles of Solidarity

Recognition. Solidarity begins with seeing the other as a human being like oneself—with needs, desires, fears, and hopes. This recognition is not abstract; it is concrete and particular. It is not about recognizing “humanity in general” but about recognizing this person, these people, in their specific situation.

Reciprocity. Solidarity involves mutual giving and receiving. It is not charity, which maintains hierarchy by placing giver above receiver. It is not exchange, which reduces relationship to transaction. It is the ongoing circulation of care, support, and acknowledgment that sustains all parties.

Mutual commitment. Solidarity means standing with others through difficulty. It is not conditional on immediate benefit. It is not contingent on agreement about everything. It is the commitment to remain in relationship even when it is costly or inconvenient.

Autonomy within relationship. Solidarity does not dissolve individuals into a collective. It preserves the distinctness of each while affirming their connection. The goal is not fusion but differentiation in relationship—each person fully themselves, fully connected.

Voluntary association. Solidarity cannot be coerced. It must be chosen. This is why states cannot produce solidarity through law, nor markets through contract. Solidarity emerges from free persons recognizing their interdependence and acting on that recognition.

Local knowledge. Solidarity operates through direct knowledge of particular others and particular situations. It cannot be administered from a distance. It requires presence, attention, and responsiveness to context.

The Conditions of Solidarity

Solidarity is not automatically present. It requires conditions in which it can flourish:

Material sufficiency. People who are starving, freezing, or desperately insecure have limited capacity for solidarity. They may be forced into competition for survival. Solidarity requires that basic needs be met—not through charity, but through mutual provision that ensures no one falls below the threshold of viability.

Stable relationships. Solidarity grows through repeated interaction over time. It requires the opportunity to know others, to build trust, to develop shared history. Transient populations, disrupted communities, and atomized individuals struggle to sustain it.

Cultural validation. Solidarity requires cultural narratives that value relationship, reciprocity, and mutual care. When the dominant culture celebrates competition, accumulation, and individual success, solidarity becomes counter-cultural—possible but difficult.

Institutional space. Solidarity needs room to operate—physical spaces where people can gather, social spaces where relationships can develop, institutional spaces where mutual aid can be organized. When these spaces are colonized by state or market, solidarity is squeezed out.

Protection from predation. Solidarity is vulnerable to those who would exploit it. The generous can be taken advantage of. The trusting can be betrayed. Solidarity requires mechanisms—informal but effective—to protect against predation without recreating hierarchy.

The Fruits of Solidarity

When solidarity flourishes, it produces outcomes that neither individualism nor communism can reliably deliver:

Resilience. Solidarity-based communities are remarkably resilient. When crisis strikes, they mobilize. When individuals falter, they are held. When external systems fail, they continue. The disaster research documenting “therapeutic communities” is evidence of this resilience.

Well-being. Humans are social animals. We need connection, recognition, and belonging. Solidarity provides these. The extensive literature on social determinants of health confirms that social connection is among the strongest predictors of physical and mental well-being.

Meaning. Solidarity provides a framework of meaning that transcends individual achievement or collective obligation. It is the meaning of being part of something larger than oneself while remaining fully oneself—of contributing to the flourishing of those one loves and who love one in return.

Freedom. Solidarity offers a different kind of freedom than individualism: not freedom from relationship, but freedom through relationship. In strong communities, individuals are free to be themselves because they are held, not because they are alone.

Sustainability. Solidarity-based societies tend to be more sustainable because they value relationship over accumulation, sufficiency over growth, and long-term flourishing over short-term gain. They do not require endless expansion to maintain their internal logic.

Part Five: The Energy Landscape

Mapping the Configurations

We can now map the three configurations on an energy landscape, with the psychopathic gradient indicated:

Energy
  ^
  |                                    Communism (High Energy)
  |                                   (Collectivist Psychopathy)
  |
  |                          Individualism (High Energy)
  |                        (Individualist Psychopathy)
  |
  |                Solidarity (Lowest Energy)
  |              (Psychopathy-Suppressing)
  |
  +--------------------------------------------> Social Complexity
         Low                              High

The vertical axis represents the energy required to maintain the configuration. The horizontal axis represents social complexity—scale, differentiation, coordination.

Solidarity is the lowest point on this landscape. It is the basin of attraction toward which human societies tend when external energy inputs are removed. It requires the least work to maintain because it aligns with fundamental human capacities evolved over hundreds of thousands of years. It also tends to suppress psychopathic behavior because psychopathy is costly in dense networks of mutual recognition.

Individualism and communism are higher-energy configurations. They require continuous input to maintain. They can persist for long periods, but only through constant work against the gradient. They also tend to select for psychopathic traits in their elites and to normalize psychopathic behavior in their populations through socioeconomic patterns echoing.

When energy inputs falter—when growth slows, when coercion weakens, when ideology loses credibility—these configurations tend to slide back toward solidarity. And as they slide, the psychopathic echo fades.

The Gradient

The gradient is the natural human tendency toward relationship. It is:

  • The infant’s reaching for connection
  • The friend’s offering of support
  • The stranger’s impulse to help
  • The community’s gathering in crisis
  • The soldier’s recognition across enemy lines

This gradient is not a moral force. It is not “good” in some cosmic sense. It is simply the direction of least resistance—the path of least energy expenditure for human social organization. And it is also the direction away from psychopathy, because psychopathy is a strategy that works only when relationships are thin and exit is easy.

The gradient is why individualism requires constant reinforcement through media, education, and law. Left to themselves, people form relationships, share resources, and care for each other. The individualist must be constantly reminded that they are supposed to be self-interested, that dependence is failure, that connection is optional. This reminder is the echo—the culture working to normalize psychopathy.

The gradient is why communism requires constant coercion through party, police, and propaganda. Left to themselves, people form their own associations, pursue their own purposes, and resist being dissolved into the collective. The communist must be constantly reminded that they belong to something larger, that individual desire is suspect, that deviation is betrayal. This reminder is also the echo—a different channel for the same normalization.

The gradient is why solidarity requires only that the fog be lifted. It is what remains when the machines stop running. It is what emerges when the echo fades.

The Fog

The fog is the great enabler of high-energy configurations. It is:

  • The propaganda that makes enemies of neighbors
  • The ideology that justifies exploitation
  • The abstraction that renders specific humans invisible
  • The distraction that focuses attention away from what matters
  • The complexity that obscures simple truths
  • The normalization that makes psychopathy seem virtuous

The fog requires energy to maintain. It must be constantly generated, renewed, and thickened. When it lifts—as in the Christmas Truce—what emerges is solidarity.

This is why authorities worked so hard to suppress the Truce. It was not just about military discipline. It was about maintaining the fog. The Truce threatened to reveal that the entire edifice of the war—the nations, the causes, the enmities—rested on a foundation that could dissolve at any moment. It threatened to expose the psychopathic normalization for what it was.

Part Six: The Historical Trajectory

The Long Arc

Human history can be understood as a series of experiments in energy configuration and psychopathic selection.

For most of our species’ existence, we lived in solidarity-based societies—small bands of hunter-gatherers organized through direct relationship, reciprocal exchange, and egalitarian norms. These societies required minimal energy to maintain because they aligned with our evolved capacities. They also kept psychopathy in check through dense networks of mutual knowledge and the threat of exclusion.

The agricultural revolution introduced surplus, which created the conditions for hierarchy. Surplus required management. Management required specialization. Specialization required inequality. The first states emerged to coordinate and protect the surplus, extracting energy from producers to maintain the new configuration.

This was the birth of high-energy society. And with it came the first large-scale opportunity for psychopathic elites. Those who could manipulate, exploit, and kill without remorse could rise in the new hierarchies. The echo began.

The great civilizations of antiquity were high-energy configurations sustained through immense effort. They selected for psychopathic traits in their ruling classes and normalized those traits through ideology, law, and culture. The pharaoh who built pyramids with slave labor. The emperor who killed rivals without compunction. The general who slaughtered entire cities. These were not exceptions. They were the ideal types of their systems.

And they rose and fell as energy inputs fluctuated. When the center could no longer extract enough energy to maintain the periphery, the system collapsed—and society slid back toward solidarity, reorganizing at local levels until the cycle began again.

The modern era intensified this dynamic. Capitalism created an individualist configuration that required constant growth to maintain and that selected for individualist psychopathy. Communism created a collectivist configuration that required constant mobilization to maintain and that selected for collectivist psychopathy. Both were unprecedented in their energy demands. Both extracted from the natural world and from human relationship on a scale never before attempted. Both generated echoes that saturated their societies.

Both are now faltering. Growth slows. Mobilization exhausts. The costs of maintaining high-energy configurations become unbearable. The fog thins. The echo fades. And beneath it, solidarity waits.

The Present Moment

We are living through a period of transition. The old configurations are failing, but the new has not yet been born. The result is confusion, conflict, and the desperate attempt to maintain the fog by any means necessary.

Individualism produces loneliness, inequality, and ecological collapse. Its promises of freedom and prosperity ring hollow for more and more people. Its institutions—markets, states, legal systems—seem incapable of addressing the crises they have created. Its echo—the normalization of psychopathy—has become so pervasive that many cannot imagine any other way.

Communism produces authoritarianism, stagnation, and the suppression of difference. Its promises of liberation and equality have been betrayed so often that they no longer convince. Its surviving forms are either oppressive or irrelevant. Its echo—the collectivist psychopathy of party and state—has been exposed for what it is.

The gradient pulls toward solidarity. In the cracks of the failing systems, solidarity re-emerges—mutual aid networks, cooperative enterprises, Indigenous resurgence, community land trusts, gift economies, intentional communities. These are not yet a new configuration. They are the first shoots after fire.

And in these cracks, the psychopathic echo weakens. People recognize each other again. They remember what it is to be human together.

Part Seven: The Choice

What Is to Be Done?

The conventional answers to this question are exhausted. Revolution from above produces new forms of domination. Reform from within is absorbed and neutralized. Individual exit leaves the system unchanged.

The framework developed here suggests a different answer: work in the cracks. Build solidarity where it can be built. Create spaces where different rules apply. Weave networks of mutual aid that can expand when the system falters. Make the fog visible so others can see through it. Demonstrate that another way is possible by living it. Break the echo by creating spaces where it cannot reach.

This is not a grand strategy. It is not a blueprint for victory. It is an orientation—a commitment to the gradient, to the natural tendency, to what remains when the machines stop.

The Role of Tolerance

The organic socio-economic systems proposal offers a specific application of this orientation. It asks for nothing but tolerance—permission for communities to self-organize using their own resources, on their own terms.

This is not a demand. It is an invitation. It offers decision-makers a solution to a problem they cannot solve: what to do with populations the system cannot incorporate. It costs them nothing. It contains failure locally. It offers potential upside through innovation and reduced burden.

Tolerance is cheap. It requires no new spending, no legislative battles, no ideological conversion. It simply requires not interfering with those who wish to build something different.
